findings.title: Hardcoded default CSRF secret ships verbatim into every scaffolded app
findings.description: starterAuthCsrf.secret is the literal 'replace-with-a-deployed-secret' (L59). This value is the HMAC-SHA256 key used to mint and validate ALL CSRF tokens for the scaffolded app: csrfToken/validateCsrfToken in packages/server/src/csrf.ts compute createHmac('sha256', options.secret).update(sessionId). I confirmed the create-jiso scaffolder does NOT replace it: renderTemplate (packages/create-jiso/src/index.ts:125) only substitutes {{mustache}} tokens, and this secret is a plain string literal, so it is copied verbatim. There is no process.env read, no generated random secret, no .env.example, and neither templates/README.md nor templates/docs/deployment.md mentions replacing it. Because the value is published in the create-jiso package, it is attacker-known. The stateless synchronizer-token design relies on the HMAC key being secret; with a public key the scheme's security collapses to the secrecy/predictability of the session id (request.session.id) or, for the unauthenticated login form, request.authCsrfId. If a session id is ever exposed (logs, URLs, API payloads) or authCsrfId is predictable/attacker-influenceable, an attacker can compute a valid token = HMAC-SHA256('replace-with-a-deployed-secret', id) and forge CSRF requests, defeating the protection on the sign-in and sign-out mutations that reference starterAuthCsrf. Note: app-shell.ts already demonstrates the correct pattern of reading deploy config from process.env.
findings.recommendation: Do not hardcode the secret. Read it from the environment and fail closed if unset, e.g. `const secret = process.env.JISO_CSRF_SECRET; if (!secret) throw new Error('JISO_CSRF_SECRET is required')`. Alternatively, have the create-jiso scaffolder generate a unique random secret per project (crypto.randomBytes) and write it to a generated .env, and document the requirement in deployment.md/README.

findings.title: Login open redirect via `next` (backslash bypass of redirect validation)
findings.description: The starter login recipe renders an attacker-controllable `next` value into the sign-in form (renderLoginForm L119: <input type="hidden" name="next" value={options.next ?? '/cart'} />) and configures the sign-in mutation with defaultRedirectTo (L81). On submit, `next` is parsed by betterAuthSignInEmailInput (next: optionalStringSchema, which only checks 'is a string') and passed to redirectPath(input.next, options.defaultRedirectTo ?? '/') in the sign-in handler. The returned value becomes the HTTP `Location:` header of a 303 redirect (confirmed: packages/server/src/mutation.ts:639-646 sets Location from redirectTo). The validator redirectPath (packages/better-auth/src/index.ts:1204-1209) rejects values not starting with '/' and rejects values starting with '//', but it does NOT reject a leading backslash: `/\evil.com` passes (startsWith('/') is true, startsWith('//') is false). Per the WHATWG URL spec, browsers normalize backslashes to forward slashes in the authority position, so `Location: /\evil.com` resolves to the scheme-relative `//evil.com` -> https://evil.com. An attacker can send a victim a link like /login?next=/\evil.com (the route handler populating options.next from the query string); after the victim authenticates, they are redirected off-site, enabling phishing/credential or token theft. The root-cause fix belongs in redirectPath, but the starter recipe is the reachable, user-facing surface.
findings.recommendation: Harden the redirect allowlist used by the sign-in/sign-up mutations (redirectPath in @jiso/better-auth): after confirming a leading '/', also reject values whose second character is '/' OR '\\' (and reject control/whitespace and encoded variants), or parse with `new URL(next, base)` and require the resolved origin to equal the app origin. Prefer an explicit path allowlist for post-login destinations.
